speaker: interviewer
text: OK. It's the 18th of December 1995. The survivor is Pepa Rosenman. The interviewer is Susi Phillips. We're in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia. And today's language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Susi Phillips doing an interview with Pepa Rosenman on December the 18th, 1995 in Sydney, Australia. The interview will be undertaken in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell us your name, spelling your surname.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Pepa Rosenman. R-O-S-E-N-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell us when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born the fourth of September 1914 in Przemysl, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old are you now? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you please just tell us about your family, your parents, brothers, sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were quite a large family. It means that I had-- we were six children at home, three girls and three boys. I was the one-- the fifth one. Afterwards was a-- a boy, a young brother. And my mother and my sister-- and my father and the grandparents from both sides. So it was quite a large gathering when we were all together. You want [INAUDIBLE]-- my eldest sister Ella was quite older-- 10 years older than I was. Then was my brother, Joseph. Then was my sister, Rosa, my brother, David, then was-- I was the fifth one, and Henry, the youngest brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were at home, quite comfortable. We lived in quite comfortable circumstances. The parents-- the family was kosher, very kosher, very traditional. It was not orthodox. We lived normally, like, normal dress. But the only thing is that at home, we were really not so much religious as traditional. My father told his prayers every day, and-- and we took it for granted that-- nobody was talking about Jewishness or not [INAUDIBLE]. That was our normal life. And so it went till the first-- when I went to the primary school. Then I realized that I'm different than other children because the first day in primary school, the teacher started to read our names. And she put the Jewish kids on the one side and the other on the other side, which I did not understand at this time why. You see, what is the reason? But she redacted it very quickly the next day. Must have been a pressure on her somehow.

speaker: interviewee
text: The second time when I realized that I am different that I got very friendly in the first year of primary school with two girls. And we played around and [INAUDIBLE] in school, and we liked each other. So they invited me once to their home. And we spent a very lovely afternoon. The next day, they told me, we are very sorry, but you can't come-- we can't play with you anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: For me, it was something new. Why? The mother told them that they can't play with me, and they can't invite me because I'm Jewish. And that was when I really felt different. From this day on, somehow instinctively, I was clinging to my Jewish friends, to the Jewish environm-- environment, and I knew that it exist, especially when I went to school, the Polish school. But I did not-- I tried not to mix with them. I was so hurt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we all went, of course, to Polish schools, all kids. And that is where the first breach in the tradition come-- came in because we started to write on Shabbat. We excited to go to school on Shabbat. Other-- all other holy days-- Jewish holy days-- we were strictly observing at home. But Shabbat, we had to go to school. Then at 10, I finished the primary school, and I-- I had to pass an exam to go to the high school. That was the way that it was in my time, and it is in Poland at 10. So I passed the exam. I went to high school, again, to the Polish high school-- the same high school which one sister and the other sister went before. That-- When I was 11-- around second year-- I was approached by some older girls to join an organization, Zionist youth movement, Hashomer Hatzair. I joined it because it was normal in our family. All my brothers and sisters went through the same organization. I want to explain that Hashomer Hatzair was then the most popular and the strongest youth-- youth movement. It was based on the principle of a scout. It means we had all those activities as a scout movement. But apart from that, we had ideological background. It means it was Zionist and socialist. Zionist, we-- our movement was directed to build Palestine. It means a home for Jews in Palestine, but it had to be based on socialist ideol-- ideology.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was a very happy part-- time for me. We-- apart from what we learned in school, we broadened our mind very much in this organization where we started to think about social issue, Jewish issues, and so on. Then-- Pepa, before you go on--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --too much further, I'd just like to take you back a little bit to your family life. Could you just describe and tell us about your parents, what you father did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Good. My father was a very successful businessmen. My mother was working most of the time with him. And he was very often on the [? wire ?] because he had this big business with those-- big business all over the Poland. But he made always a point on Friday afternoon that he was at home because that was when Shabbat started. Then my mother was a very unusual and very exceptional woman. She was-- at this time, she was-- she was very young when she married my father. But she was helping in the business full-time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: The business was metals-- new and old iron, old building materials. And then my father brought a machine to make-- you know, in those times, when you wanted to send a parcel or a case or something, you had to seal it with a lead seal. And he was making those. He was manufacturing those lead seals. So it was quite a large business and different-- different directions. And my mother was helping on the spot when my mother-- father had to go traveling. And we were at home always, but we had always servants-- maid, cook, handyman around the house, around the properties. The children-- all of us-- we went to private schools. Only one went to high school, public high school. We were all-- we had all private lessons-- music lessons, languages, and whatever it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages did you learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned German because German was the language most popular in our part of Poland. Our part was-- before the war-- under Austrian occupation, and that was the official language. So all-- my parents, and the whole family, and the family which was brought in come to visit us, we all spoke German. So it was much easier for me after I was in school because the German was our second language. I chose as a German. Then we had-- as it was a very big difference between the ages of my sisters and brothers-- so my eldest sister, for example, she acted as a-- really as a mother because she was there always. And my mother was in the business. So even when she was married, I got used to it. When I needed something, I wrote to her or I told her that I need something. I did not go to my mother-- only to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: But on the other hand, we were not as close as siblings should be because the difference of ages and the difference, for example, with this sister, Rosa who is st-- living in Los Angeles, it was 4 and 1/2 years difference. When I was started only the high school, she was on a higher level. She was nearly finishing. You see? So we had not too much in common. Then she left to Czechoslovakia-- Prague-- for her study. So then we did not see each other again. So really speaking, we got close after the war when we found each other. And of course, the brother-- who is still now living in Paris-- he left Przemysl and Poland, stayed after the high school to study in France. And he never come back. He survived the war in France. He was, at the beginning, in the army and then in the unoccupied zone. Then what you want me? About your parents, did they have political affiliations?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my father. First of all, our family was always Zionist oriented. It was-- as long as I remember, it was a Zionist box at home. It was all Zionist [INAUDIBLE]. Officially, when my father was voting, he voted socialist. That was a very-- thing-- a thing which was very Przemysl because it was the first and only Jewish member of parliament from Przemysl, Lieberman, which was-- he was socially-- socialist. So all the Jews voted, not because he was socialist or not, but because he was the first member of parliament, Jewish parliament. So they were called the [? Lieberman ?] People. But as I told you, we were very Jewish and Zionist oriented, the whole family, always. And my father liked all the things which were new. He did not have any formal education, not in those times. But he was intelligent enough to be interested in, for example, from one-- once from a travel, he brought a beautiful encyclopedia, German encyclopedia. Or he was bringing books on when was-- in our time come to Jewish theater from Wilno. I was then a young girl. He took me to see-- and I will never forget. I saw And I did not understand Yiddish, but I will still-- I still see Ida Kaminska on-- on the stage.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that-- so he really was giving us everything what he missed in his life if it comes to education and interest in music and-- for example, he brought one of the first radios he brought once home. Still was this big tube like and his master [? voice ?] pictures, you know? So we were a very enlightened family-- even my grandfather, who was more on the religious side. I will not tell that he was really orthodox, but he was more strictly religious. Even he spoke to us Polish, for example. And he was talking-- telling us-- he was interested in all of-- he was, for example, interested very much in music. He was coming Saturday, although the orthodox are not allowed to play Saturday. But he gave me the [INAUDIBLE] to play for him because he had no time the whole week. So he came on Saturday afternoon just to listen a little bit to the music I played for him on the piano. Do you still play the piano?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't play the piano. I did not touch it from the day that the Russian came and took away my pianos and all my sheet music. It was in 1939, and of course, with everything what we went through, that was the last on my mind too. But I was quite good at it. At 14, my teacher told my father that she can't-- they can't give me anymore in Przemysl-- that was quite a small town-- that he should send me to the conservatorium of music to Lwow. Well, it was quite time ago, and my father told, you don't send a child to a big town alone. And that was it. And that was [INAUDIBLE]. But I played till-- at home for my own pleasure and for a time. And now I don't know if I would even remember [LAUGHS] after such a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a little bit about your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That is very important to describe the town because it has a historical significance afterwards and during-- you will hear afterwards, during the war. Przemysl is situated in the south in the center between Lwow and Krakaow. It is divided by a River San. It was a very nice town, really nice. Whoever talks even know about Przemysl-- it is a little bit-- was a little bit like here, Sydney, on the hill because we were very close to Karpaty. It was a very hilly country.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had, for example, the left bank was flat. That was the Polish side. The right bank was on the hills with the all the royal castle and all the main buildings, like town hall, synagogues, churches, and everything. That was the main part of the town. That was where-- where most of the Jews lived and Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were divided nearly evenly between those three nationalities. Like we were 20-- 20-odd thousand Polish people, 23,000 Jews, and 20-odd thousand Ukrainian. They lived mostly on the edge of the town. In the center was the old Jewish quarter. And most of the Jews who were business people-- they had shops. They had [INAUDIBLE], and professions-- professions like lawyers and this. They were in the center of the town. I mentioned that specially, that-- the River San because afterwards when the Russian and Stalin and Hitler divided Poland between themselves, San was the border. On the left side, the Polish side, were Germans, and on our side, we belonged to-- we were under Stalin occupation. So and the river, which divided us, was very easy to cross. The bridge was still standing. Anyway, the tine-- town was quite peaceful for a long time, perhaps because it was such a balance between all those three nationalities. But we did not really know much of the tension between till the two, three years before the war when the pressure of the anti-Semitism, which came from Germany on a-- somehow it did. It was so strong that you could-- you-- you could see the pickets in front of the Jewish shops. Don't buy from the Jews or something like that. And when we started really to feel the government pressure on fiscal and economic on the Jews. But till-- till then, it was quite peaceful. We had, for example, as a Jewish community of 20-odd thousand people, we had our own institution. We had our own council, which was called Kahal, with our president. And we had our schools. We had our hospital, Jewish hospital. We had our bath house, yes, and of course, all the synagogues. And one of the oldest in Poland-- a beautiful synagogue-- was just opposite us where we lived at the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we lived peacefully. For example, when we went-- I will tell you, even in the county, that is what-- afterwards, you don't understand how the people could change because when I was a kid and we were in Hashomer Hatzair, for example, we went hiking all over the country. And we were singing Jewish songs, Hebrew songs. And we had the flag was a-- Magen Dav-- David. And the peasants not only did not hurt us, but they were always friendly. They invited us in, and-- and that is how it is really difficult to understand how they changed when the pressure came on the Jews. Anyway what do you want to know yet about Przemysl? Yes. Well, tell us about your school days. You told us you went to primary school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went to primary school. And after the-- those two incidents, which hurt me, everything went quiet, and not-- not only quiet, but the teacher who's tried to divide us in the beginning was very friendly with us because we-- because we were mostly the good pupils, you know, the Jewish kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I finish it at 10, as I told you. We-- we were-- somehow after this first incident, the Jewish girls kept together without any pressure. You see? And we get very friendly. We had a group of seven, eight-- [INAUDIBLE]. Seven-- seven, eight girls, and we were very, very close and friendly. Then I went to the Polish high school where, again, the majority was Jewish because the Polish girls had a school-- high school where the Jews were-- Jewish girls were not permitted. It was a religious Catholic school. So here, were mostly Jewish. There were a few-- and it was again, the school-- we were all-- most of us-- it means my eldest sister and my eldest brother, Ella and Joseph, were not very studious. It means they did not have the-- the aptitude to-- to learn higher-- on higher [INAUDIBLE]. They were more practical. Like my Ella was sent to a finishing school, and she was an excellent hostess and cook and whatever-- a young lady, still now.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother went to business, the oldest one. But from Rosa down, we were all excellent in schools-- school. Rosa, for example, was a special-- her special subjects were mathematics and physics. And my brother, the same. And my inclination was towards languages, history, and things like this, rather humanistic. And we were all very good at school. It means we finished ever year with distinctions and our matriculation was with distinction. And the problem started when my sister Rosa wanted to go to university. She wanted to study medicine, and that was the problem in Poland. Jews were not allowed. There were a few here and there-- you know, you could count them on-- on one hand-- who somehow through some means, got admitted. But on the whole, every Jew who wanted to study medicine had to go to a foreign country. Like some went to Italy. Some went to France. And my sister and a lot of them went to Czechoslovakia. It was the closest one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she went to Prague. And she finished at there, the-- her study. Then two years later, my brother wanted to study. And he wanted to study engineering. Perhaps he could have been admitted, but in Poland, was like that. You could be admitted to some faculties like art, law, even engineering. But once you finished, there was no hope for a job. That's one thing. And on the other hand, the universities were not very safe places for Jews, for Jewish students, especially engineering and veterinary. There were pogroms. There were-- now, very often, they threw the-- the Jew-- Jewish students out, you know? It happened that one of our friends was thrown from the window, and he never regained his brain. He was damaged for his whole life. So my brother decided to go to France, which he did. And he studied there, finished, and he never come back to Poland. Although my parents wanted to give him anything to come back, he decided that he doesn't want. He stayed there. He went to the army at the beginning of the war. And then when France lost the war and he was released, he went to the unoccupied-- unoccupied zone and survived there. And then was my turn. By then, I was the only one from the six children to be at home. The older married, left. The youngest, my brother, he left. I was the only one. And as I told you, at this time, the pressure on my parents, who started to get older, start to be very strong you know? And my-- Pepa, we'll just stop here for a moment, and we'll continue with the next tape. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape one of the interview with Pepa Rosenman. It's the 18th of December 1995, and we we're in Sydney, Australia.
